6.2 Das Cockpit online bereitstellen

Dein Cockpit funktioniert lokal hervorragend. Doch wenn du im Team arbeitest, wollt ihr alle auf den gleichen, aktuellen Datenstand zugreifen. Die Zentrale muss also ins Netz.

Bevor du beginnst, musst du eine wichtige Grundregel des Internets kennen: Möchtest du dein Cockpit als Website aufrufen, musst du deine Hauptdatei zwingend in index.html umbenennen. Webserver sind so programmiert, dass sie exakt diese Datei als Startseite laden. Heißt deine Datei anders (etwa cockpit.html), erkennt der Server sie nicht und dein Bildschirm bleibt leer.

Nun stehen wir vor einem branchenbekannten Problem: Das reine Hochladen einer Webseite ist heute oft kostenlos – aber sobald man ein sicheres „Türschloss“ (einen Passwortschutz) davorlegen möchte, verlangen fast alle Anbieter plötzlich Geld.

Eine extrem wichtige Warnung vorab: Wenn du eine KI fragst, wie du eine HTML-Datei kostenlos mit einem Passwort schützt, rät sie dir oft zu einem kleinen JavaScript-Code, der beim Öffnen ein Passwort-Fenster einblendet. Tu das niemals! Das ist ein reiner „Fake-Schutz“. Jeder, der im Browser auf „Rechtsklick -> Seitenquelltext anzeigen“ klickt, sieht sofort alle deine Finanz- und Prozessdaten sowie das Passwort im Klartext.

Eine extrem wichtige Warnung vorab: Wenn du eine KI fragst, wie du eine HTML-Datei kostenlos mit einem Passwort schützt, rät sie dir oft zu einem kleinen JavaScript-Code, der beim Öffnen ein Passwort-Fenster einblendet. Tu das niemals! Das ist ein reiner „Fake-Schutz“. Jeder, der im Browser auf „Rechtsklick -> Seitenquelltext anzeigen“ klickt, sieht sofort alle deine Finanz- und Prozessdaten sowie das Passwort im Klartext.

Um dein Cockpit wirklich sicher und kostenlos für dein Team bereitzustellen, gibt es diese Lösungswege, je nach deinem technischen Komfortbedürfnis:

6.2.1 Der digitale Tresor (GitHub für 0 Euro)

GitHub ist die weltweit größte Plattform für Softwareprojekte. Für No-Coder ist das unsere erste Wahl, wenn es um den reinen Datenaustausch geht.
Bei kostenlosen GitHub-Accounts kannst du Dateien extrem sicher und privat in der Cloud speichern.

Schritt 1 (Tresor anlegen): Erstelle ein neues Projekt (ein sogenanntes „Repository“) und stelle es zwingend auf „Private“. Damit ist alles vor fremdem Zugriff geschützt.

Schritt 2 (Team einladen): Unter den Einstellungen („Settings > Collaborators“) lädst du deine Kollegen per E-Mail ein. Nur sie erhalten den Schlüssel zum Tresor.

Schritt 3 (Der Workflow): Deine Kollegen loggen sich bei GitHub ein, laden sich die aktuelle Cockpit-Datei herunter und öffnen sie per Doppelklick lokal auf ihrem Rechner.

Wichtiger Hinweis: Nutze bei GitHub niemals die Funktion „GitHub Pages“! Auch bei einem privaten Projekt veröffentlicht diese Funktion deine Seite für jeden sichtbar im offenen Internet. Die Zugangskontrolle für Webseiten ("Access Control for GitHub Pages") ist ausschließlich im Tarif GitHub Enterprise Cloud (ca. 20€ monatlich) verfügbar.

Fazit: 0 Euro, 100 % DSGVO-konform. GitHub dient hier als hochsicherer, digitaler Firmen-USB-Stick.

6.2.2 Cloudflare für 0 Euro – Live im Netz

Wenn das ständige Herunterladen nervt und dein Team einen echten Live-Link (z. B. dein-cockpit.pages.dev) im Browser aufrufen soll, nutzen wir einen Trick: Wir kombinieren zwei kostenlose Dienste des Anbieters Cloudflare (cloudflare.com):

Cloudflare Pages: Hier lädst du deine Dateien einfach per Drag-and-Drop hoch. Cloudflare macht daraus kostenlos eine rasend schnelle Webseite.

Cloudflare Zero Trust: Das ist unser Türsteher. Diese Konzern-Sicherheitsfunktion ist für bis zu 50 Nutzer komplett gratis. Du legst im Menü fest, dass nur bestimmte E-Mail-Adressen (z. B. @deinefirma.de) auf die Seite zugreifen dürfen.

Die Umsetzung

Da Cloudflare eigentlich für IT-Profis gebaut wurde, wirken die Menüs auf den ersten Blick etwas einschüchternd. Aber keine Sorge, folge einfach diesem Rezept:

Das Hosting: Erstelle dir einen kostenlosen Account auf cloudflare.com. Klicke im Menü links auf „Workers & Pages“ und wähle „Upload assets“ (Dateien hochladen). Ziehe deinen Ordner mit der index.html in das Feld. Cloudflare generiert nun deinen Live-Link (z. B. https://cockpit-abc.pages.dev).

Zero Trust aktivieren: Klicke im linken Hauptmenü auf „Zero Trust“. Wichtiger Hinweis: Cloudflare fragt dich hier nach einer Zahlungsmethode (Kreditkarte/PayPal) und du musst den „Free Plan“ wählen. Keine Panik! Das dient nur der Abwehr von Spam-Robotern. Es werden 0 Euro abgebucht.

Den Türsteher aufstellen: Im Zero-Trust-Menü klickst du auf „Access“ und dann auf „Applications“. Klicke auf „Add an Application“ und wähle „Self-Hosted“. Trage hier deinen vorhin erstellten Link (dein-name.pages.dev) ein.

Die Gästeliste schreiben: Im nächsten Fenster scrollst du zu den „Policies“ (den Regeln). Hier legst du fest, wer rein darf. Wähle als Aktion „Allow“ (Erlauben) und füge unter „Include“ die E-Mail-Adressen deiner Mitarbeiter ein (oder gleich eure gesamte Firmenendung, z.B. @deinefirma.de).

Testen: Speichere alles ab. Öffne nun ein „Inkognito-Fenster“ in deinem Browser und rufe deinen Link auf. Wenn du alles richtig gemacht hast, siehst du statt deines Cockpits jetzt die Abfrage: „Bitte E-Mail-Adresse eingeben, um einen Zugangscode zu erhalten.“

Tipp für No-Coder: Wenn sich Menüs bei Cloudflare in der Zwischenzeit geändert haben sollten, mach einfach einen Screenshot von der Cloudflare-Seite, lade ihn in deine KI hoch, sag du gerade nicht weiterkommst und dir wird geholfen!

Der Workflow: Ruft ein Mitarbeiter euren Link auf, ist die Seite blockiert. Er muss seine E-Mail-Adresse eintippen und erhält sofort einen PIN-Code in sein Postfach. Gibt er diesen ein, öffnet sich das Cockpit. Kein Passwort, das man sich merken oder das gehackt werden kann – absolute Sicherheit auf Enterprise-Niveau für 0 Euro!

6.2.3 Webhosting

Vielleicht hast du für deine Firmen-Webseite ohnehin schon einen Server gemietet (bei Anbietern wie IONOS, Strato oder Hostinger). In diesem Fall fallen keine neuen Kosten an.

Hochladen: Du schiebst deine Dateien über ein kostenloses Datei-Übertragungsprogramm (wie FileZilla – dein digitaler Möbelpacker) einfach auf deinen Server in einen Unterordner.

Das Türschloss: Da das Internet für jeden offen ist, musst du diesen Ordner zwingend schützen. Bei Webhostern macht man das zuverlässig mit einer winzigen Textdatei namens .htaccess.

Tipp für No-Coder: Frag einfach deine KI: „Ich habe einen Webspace. Schreibe mir den .htaccess-Code, um meinen Cockpit-Ordner mit dem Passwort 'Geheim123' zu schützen, und erkläre mir Schritt für Schritt, wie und wo ich diese Datei anlegen muss.“

6.2.4 Selbst hosten (Die Nerd-Route)

6.2.4 Selbst hosten (Die Nerd-Route)

Du willst deine Daten in keiner Cloud wissen, auch nicht bei Cloudflare oder deinem Webhoster? Dann musst du selbst zum Server-Anbieter werden. Ein ehrlicher Hinweis vorab: An diesem Punkt verlassen wir den reinen No-Code-Bereich und wagen uns in die Welt der Netzwerke vor. Lass dir hierbei jeden Schritt von der KI erklären!

Variante A: Vom eigenen Laptop hosten (Das Firmen-WLAN)
Kannst du dein Cockpit direkt von deinem Arbeits-Laptop aus freigeben? Ja. Du nutzt dafür den Python-Trick aus Kapitel 5 (den Befehl python -m http.server 8000). Solange dieses schwarze Kommandozeilen-Fenster offen ist, sendet dein Laptop das Cockpit in euer lokales Netzwerk. Wenn der Kollege am Schreibtisch gegenüber deine IP-Adresse in seinen Browser eintippt, sieht er das Cockpit.

Achtung, hier lauern gefährliche Datenlecks:

Falscher Ordner: Starte diesen Befehl niemals in deinem Hauptordner (wie „Eigene Dateien“). Jeder im WLAN kann sonst über den Browser deine privaten Rechnungen oder Urlaubsfotos herunterladen! Erstelle immer einen isolierten Ordner nur für das Cockpit.

Falsches WLAN: Nutze diesen Trick nur im sicheren Büro-WLAN. Machst du das im ICE, einem Café oder einem offenen Gäste-Netzwerk, präsentierst du deine Firmenzahlen für jeden Fremden im gleichen Netz auf dem Silbertablett.
(Zusatz-Warnung: Versuche niemals, deinen Heim-Router so umzuprogrammieren, dass Kollegen über das offene Internet auf deinen PC zugreifen können. Damit reißt du ein Loch in deine Firewall, über das sich Hacker freuen!)

Variante B: Der Raspberry Pi (Die Königsdisziplin)
Wenn du die volle Kontrolle willst, aber deinen Arbeitsrechner nicht 24 Stunden am Tag laufen lassen möchtest, kaufst du dir einen Raspberry Pi (ca. 80 Euro). Das ist ein vollwertiger Minicomputer in der Größe einer EC-Karte.

Du schließt ihn an deinen Router an, installierst einen Webserver und legst dein Cockpit darauf ab. Mit Tools wie „Tailscale“ (einem extrem anfängerfreundlichen, kostenlosen Netzwerk-Tool) spannst du dann eine Art unsichtbares Kabel zwischen dem Laptop deines Mitarbeiters im Homeoffice und deinem Raspberry Pi im Büro.

Fazit: Einmalige Hardware-Kosten, 0 Euro monatlich. Es erfordert etwas Einarbeitung mit der KI, aber es ist das ultimative Projekt für frisch gebackene System-Architekten, die 100 % Datenkontrolle wollen!

6.3. Automatisierung und Agenten

Wenn du Blut geleckt hast und dich in außerhalb des No-Code Bereiches wohl fühlst ist dieser Abschnitt dein nächster logischer Schritt. Du musst das jetzt noch nicht umsetzen, aber du solltest wissen, was möglich ist. Dies ist also nur ein Teaser, keine Schritt-für-Schritt-Anleitung.

Bisher ziehst du Dateien per Drag-and-Drop in dein Cockpit. Was wäre, wenn die Daten ganz von alleine dorthin wandern und verarbeitet werden? Hier kommen Automatisierungs-Tools wie n8n (n8n.io) ins Spiel, die als unsichtbare Helfer im Hintergrund agieren. Mit autonomen Agenten wie OpenClaw (github.com/openclaw) erreicht Automatisierung derzeit die höchste Evolutionsstufe. Während klassische Systeme wie n8n „stur“ vorgegebene Befehlsketten abarbeiten, agieren diese KI-Agenten eigenständig: Sie können Support-Tickets inhaltlich klassifizieren, proaktiv bei Lieferanten nachhaken oder Termine samt Fahrzeiten selbstständig koordinieren.

OpenClaw als dein digitaler Webmaster: Wenn du dein Cockpit wie zuvor beschrieben auf GitHub abgelegt hast, entfaltet OpenClaw sein wahres Potenzial. Du musst Daten nicht mehr händisch exportieren und hochladen.

Stell dir vor: Ein neuer Handwerker-Beleg kommt per E-Mail rein. Der OpenClaw-Agent liest die Mail, extrahiert die Kosten, rechnet sie der richtigen Kostenstelle zu und schreibt die neue Summe direkt in die CSV-Datei deines GitHub-Repositories. Anschließend führt der Agent völlig autonom einen sogenannten „Commit & Push“ aus – er speichert die Änderung quasi offiziell ab und lädt sie selbstständig hoch.

GitHub speichert die aktualisierte CSV-Datei. Beim nächsten Aufruf liest deine cockpit.html diese Datei aus und zeigt die aktualisierten Diagramme an. Wenn du oder deine Mitarbeiter das nächste Mal die Seite öffnen, sind die Diagramme bereits auf dem neuesten Stand. Keine manuelle Eingabe, keine Wartezeit. 

Diese vollautomatisierte Git-Integration bringt echtes Enterprise-Architecture-Niveau in deine Projekte – fast so nahtlos und unsichtbar. Kurz gesagt: Die KI erledigt die gesamte Vor- und Nacharbeit.

Aber Vorsicht:

Echte Autonomie bringt nicht nur Freiheit, sondern birgt auch erhebliche Sicherheitsrisiken. Ein Agent, der eigenständig E-Mails liest und Code veröffentlicht, erfordert ein sauberes Setup. Hier verlässt du die Anfängerzone und musst technisch deutlich tiefer in die Materie einsteigen. Wenn deine Neugierde geweckt ist und du sehen willst, wie die Zukunft der Automatisierung aussieht, wirf am besten einen Blick direkt in die Dokumentation: docs.openclaw.ai/start/getting-started

Dein Weg geht weiter

Du hast die Werkzeuge, du hast die KI an deiner Seite – jetzt liegt es an dir, was du daraus baust. Bleib neugierig, lerne immer tiefer in die Entwicklersphäre einzusteigen, teste Dinge aus und vor allem: Behalte die Kontrolle über deine Daten und dein Business.

Glossar

0–9

0-Euro-Cockpit: Eine selbstgebaute, kostenlose digitale Kommandozentrale für das eigene Unternehmen, die ohne teure Lizenzen auskommt und lediglich einen Webbrowser und Texteditor benötigt.

A

API (Application Programming Interface): Eine Programmierschnittstelle, die wie ein digitaler "Kellner" fungiert. Sie vermittelt Daten und Anfragen zwischen dem Dashboard und externen Diensten (wie einer KI oder Datenbank), ohne dass die Webseite neu geladen werden muss.

B

BPMN (Business Process Model and Notation): Ein weltweiter Industriestandard zur formalen und rechtssicheren Modellierung von Geschäftsprozessen. Arbeitet visuell mit sogenannten Swimlanes (Zuständigkeiten) und Gateways (Abzweigungen).

C

C4-Modell: Ein pragmatisches IT-Architektur-Modell (Context, Container, Components, Code), das IT-Landschaften und Abhängigkeiten visualisiert wie eine zoombare Landkarte – vom groben System-Kontext bis tief in den Code.

CDN (Content Delivery Network): Ein globales Netzwerk zur Auslieferung von Inhalten. Wird genutzt, um externe Code-Bibliotheken (z. B. für Diagramme) direkt in die HTML-Datei deines Cockpits einzubinden, ohne sie vorher auf die eigene Festplatte herunterladen zu müssen.

Chart.js: Eine beliebte und einsteigerfreundliche JavaScript-Bibliothek, um Zahlen in HTML-Dateien als ansprechende Torten- oder Balkendiagramme zu visualisieren.

Cockpit (vs. Dashboard): Während ein reines Dashboard Daten lediglich anzeigt, ist ein Cockpit darauf ausgelegt, Prozesse aktiv zu steuern (Kommandozentrale). Das Dashboard ist somit ein funktionaler Baustein des Cockpits.

Cockpit (vs. Dashboard): Während ein reines Dashboard Daten lediglich anzeigt, ist ein Cockpit darauf ausgelegt, Prozesse aktiv zu steuern (Kommandozentrale). Das Dashboard ist somit ein funktionaler Baustein des Cockpits.

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing): Ein Sicherheitsmechanismus im Browser. Er kann Fehlermeldungen auslösen, wenn deine lokale HTML-Datei versucht, direkt Daten von einer fremden API (wie einem Cloud-KI-Anbieter) abzugreifen.

CRM (Customer-Relationship-Management): Software zur Verwaltung von Kundenbeziehungen – z. B. für Vertrieb, Kundenservice und Kontaktpflege. Speichert zentral, wer deine Kunden sind, was sie gekauft haben und welche Kommunikation stattgefunden hat.

CSV (Comma-Separated Values): Ein simples, textbasiertes Dateiformat zur Speicherung von Tabellendaten. Ideal, um Exportdaten aus Banken oder CRM-Systemen an eine KI zu übergeben.

D

Diagram-as-Code (DaC): Eine Methode, bei der Flussdiagramme oder IT-Architekturen nicht mühsam per Maus gezeichnet, sondern in simplen Textzeilen beschrieben werden. Die Software generiert und aktualisiert das finale Bild daraus vollautomatisch.

Doc-Rot (Dokumentationsverfall): Das Phänomen, dass manuell erstellte Grafiken und Prozesse extrem schnell veralten, weil der Änderungsaufwand in Programmen wie PowerPoint im Alltag zu hoch ist.

DOM (Document Object Model): Die interne Struktur einer Webseite, wie sie der Browser „sieht". Per DOM-Manipulation kann JavaScript einzelne Elemente (z. B. eine KPI-Kachel) live verändern, ohne die gesamte Seite neu laden zu müssen.

Dropzone: Ein grafischer Bereich in deinem Cockpit, in den du Dateien (wie Excel oder CSV) einfach mit der Maus hineinziehen kannst (Drag-and-Drop), um Daten im Hintergrund zu aktualisieren.

DSGVO (Datenschutz-Grundverordnung): Europäisches Regelwerk zur Datenverarbeitung. Regelt rechtlich, welche personenbezogenen Daten dein lokales System verlassen dürfen und welche zwingend vor einem Cloud-Upload anonymisiert werden müssen.

E

EAM (Enterprise Architecture Management): Die professionelle und strukturierte Verwaltung der gesamten Unternehmensarchitektur – einschließlich Geschäftsprozessen, Datenstrukturen, Applikationslandschaft und technischer Infrastruktur.

ERP-System (Enterprise Resource Planning): Komplexe Standard-Software zur ganzheitlichen Planung von Unternehmensressourcen (Personal, Finanzen, Material). Oft verbunden mit hohen Lizenz- und Einführungskosten.

F

Fetch API: Eine eingebaute JavaScript-Funktion deines Webbrowsers, die lautlos im Hintergrund mit APIs kommuniziert, um Live-Daten abzurufen oder KI-Befehle zu senden.

H

HTML (Hypertext Markup Language): Die grundlegende Text-Auszeichnungssprache, die das Gerüst und die Struktur deines Cockpits im Webbrowser definiert.

html2canvas: Eine Bibliothek, die es ermöglicht, per Code hochauflösende Screenshots (PNGs) direkt aus den Elementen deines HTML-Cockpits zu erzeugen.

J

JSFiddle: Ein kostenloser Online-Sandkasten für Webentwickler. Perfekt für Anfänger, um generierten HTML- und JavaScript-Code direkt im Browser gefahrlos zu testen und in Echtzeit anzupassen.

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation): Ein sauberes, strukturiertes Textformat, in dem APIs und KI-Modelle ihre Datenpakete austauschen, damit dein Skript sie fehlerfrei verarbeiten und als Diagramm darstellen kann.

K

Knoten und Kanten (Nodes & Edges): Die Grundbausteine eines Wissensgraphen. Knoten sind die Akteure (Personen, Firmen, Tools), Kanten sind die Verbindungslinien, die die Beziehung zwischen ihnen darstellen.

KPI (Key Performance Indicator): Eine Kennzahl, die den Erfolg oder Fortschritt in einem bestimmten Bereich misst – z. B. Umsatz, Leerstandsquote oder offene Tickets. Im Cockpit werden KPIs als Kacheln auf dem Dashboard dargestellt.

L

LLM (Large Language Model): Großes Sprachmodell. Es bildet den technologischen Kern moderner generativer KI-Systeme (wie Google Gemini, ChatGPT oder lokal bei Ollama), das menschliche Sprache und Programmcode versteht und generiert.

M

Mermaid.js: Ein Open-Source-Tool, das simplen Text-Code direkt im Webbrowser in dynamische Flussdiagramme, Tortendiagramme oder Prozessabläufe verwandelt.

Meta-Prompting: Die Technik, eine KI darum zu bitten, den perfekten, hochprofessionellen "Master-Prompt" für eine andere Aufgabe (z. B. das Programmieren eines komplexen Dashboards) zu schreiben.

N

n8n: Ein visuelles Automatisierungs-Tool, das wie ein digitaler Fließbandarbeiter im Hintergrund Daten zwischen deinen E-Mails, KI-Modellen und Tabellen verschiebt.

No-Coder / Low-Coder: Anwender, die keine klassischen Programmiersprachen studiert haben, aber mithilfe von KI, visuellen Editoren und klaren Anweisungen komplexe Software-Lösungen bauen.

O

Ollama: Ein Programm, um leistungsstarke KI-Modelle komplett lokal und offline auf dem eigenen Rechner laufen zu lassen. Garantiert 100 % Datensicherheit bei der Verarbeitung hochsensibler Unternehmensdokumente.

OpenClaw: Ein Framework für autonome KI-Agenten, die nicht nur auf Befehle warten, sondern definierte Prozesse (wie das Klassifizieren von Tickets) selbstständig koordinieren und abarbeiten.

P

PapaParse: Eine extrem schnelle und fehlerresistente JavaScript-Bibliothek, die CSV-Dateien direkt im Browser einliest und für dein Cockpit nutzbar macht. 

PlantUML: Eine textbasierte "Diagram-as-Code"-Sprache, die besonders stark darin ist, komplexe IT-Architekturen und C4-Modelle strukturiert darzustellen.

Prompt: Der Befehl oder die genaue Arbeitsanweisung, die du der KI in den Chat tippst, um das gewünschte Ergebnis (Analyse, Code, Diagramm) zu erhalten.

S

Sankey-Diagramm: Eine spezielle Diagrammform, bei der Geld- oder Datenflüsse als unterschiedlich breite Bänder dargestellt werden. Je breiter das Band, desto größer der Betrag – ideal, um auf einen Blick zu sehen, wohin das Geld fließt.

SheetJS: Eine leistungsstarke Open-Source-Bibliothek, die es deinem Browser ermöglicht, Excel-Dateien (.xlsx) lokal zu lesen und zu verarbeiten, ohne sie auf einen Server hochzuladen

Single Source of Truth (SSOT): Ein System oder Ort (in diesem Fall das Cockpit), an dem alle gültigen Daten, Prozesse und das gesamte Firmenwissen zentral, fehlerfrei und bindend zusammenlaufen.

Swimlanes: Ein grafisches Element im BPMN-Modell ("Schwimmbahnen"). Sie unterteilen einen Prozess visuell nach den zuständigen Personen, Abteilungen oder Systemen.

Swimlanes: Ein grafisches Element im BPMN-Modell ("Schwimmbahnen"). Sie unterteilen einen Prozess visuell nach den zuständigen Personen, Abteilungen oder Systemen.

V

Vis.js: Eine mächtige JavaScript-Bibliothek, um interaktive Netzwerke und Wissensgraphen direkt im Browser darzustellen, in die man hineinzoomen und Knotenpunkte per Drag-and-Drop verschieben kann.

W

Wissensgraph (Knowledge Graph): Ein interaktives, visuelles Spinnennetz. Es macht die unsichtbaren Fäden deines Unternehmens (Wer kommuniziert mit wem? Welches Tool hat welche Abhängigkeiten?) greifbar und deckt versteckte Risiken auf.

X

XML (Extensible Markup Language): Eine Auszeichnungssprache, die Daten hierarchisch strukturiert. Wird in diesem Buch vor allem als Export-Format für Diagramm-Tools (wie draw.io) verwendet.

Spickzettel: Das 0-Euro-Cockpit

Der universelle Master-Prompt:

Ich möchte [Ziel einfügen, z. B. ein digitales Firmen-Cockpit bauen]. Schreib mir dafür den professionellen Prompt, den ich einer Programmier-KI geben kann, damit sie mir das perfekt umsetzt.

Datenschutz-Entscheidungsbaum

Frage: Wäre es schlimm, wenn diese Daten morgen in der Zeitung stehen?

JA (Bankdaten, Verträge, Personal): Lösung: Lokale KI nutzen.

Tool: Ollama (ollama.com) starten (ollama run qwen3.5:4b).

Möglichkeit 1: Anonymisieren mit Ollama, dann mit Cloud-Modellen weiterarbeiten.

Möglichkeit 2: Ausschließlich lokal arbeiten (leistungsfähige Hardware nötig).

NEIN (Marketing, allgemeine Prozesse): Lösung: Cloud-KI nutzen.

Tools: ChatGPT (chatgpt.com) oder Gemini (gemini.google.com).

Wichtig: KI-Training in den Einstellungen zwingend deaktivieren!

Diagram-as-Code: Welches Tool für was?

Anstatt zu zeichnen, beschreibst du die Struktur als Text. Die KI generiert den Code, der Browser malt das Bild.

Tipp für Design-Anpassungen: Generierten Code in diagrams.net (app.diagrams.net) importieren und per Drag-and-Drop verfeinern.

Das Cockpit-Fundament 

Dein Cockpit ist eine einzige, lokale HTML-Datei. Du bearbeitest sie ganz einfach in einem Texteditor.

Testen ohne Speichern: Nutze JSFiddle (jsfiddle.net), um Code-Änderungen in Echtzeit und absolut gefahrlos (Strg+Z rettet alles) zu testen.

Lokale Updates (Dropzone): Nutze Bibliotheken wie SheetJS und PapaParse per CDN, um CSV- und Excel-Dateien lokal im Browser einzulesen, ohne dass Daten dein System verlassen.

API-Schnittstellen (Der "Kellner"): Nutze die JavaScript fetch() Funktion, um Daten von einer KI in dein Cockpit zu holen.

JSON-Zwang: Nutze im Prompt immer den Zusatz: "Antworte AUSSCHLIESSLICH im JSON-Format, keine Einleitung."

Hosting & Teilen : 

Variante A (Sicherer Tresor): Lade dein Cockpit in ein privates GitHub-Repository hoch. Teile es nur über die „Collaborators“-Funktion mit deinem Team. Die Kollegen laden die Datei herunter und öffnen sie lokal.

Variante B (Live-Link mit Cloudflare): Für einen echten Weblink nutze Cloudflare Pages in Kombination mit Cloudflare Zero Trust.

Achtung GitHub Pages: Aktiviere GitHub Pages nur, wenn dein Cockpit absolut keine sensiblen Daten enthält (z. B. nur öffentliches Marketing). Bei kostenlosen GitHub-Accounts sind Pages-Webseiten oft öffentlich einsehbar, auch wenn das Repository privat ist!

API-Konfiguration (Base-URLs & Modelle)

Hier die wichtigsten Standard-Werte, um dein Cockpit mit dem Gehirn der KIs zu verbinden:

Ollama (Lokal - 100% Datenschutz):

Base-URL: http://localhost:11434/api/chat

Modelle (Beispiele): llama3, mistral (bzw. das lokal geladene Modell)

OpenAI (ChatGPT):

Base-URL: https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions

Modelle (Beispiele): gpt-5.4, gpt-5.4-mini

Google Gemini (via OpenAI Kompatibilitäts-API):

Base-URL: https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/chat/completions

Modelle (Beispiele): gemini-3.1-pro-preview, gemini-3-flash-preview

Quick-Troubleshooting (Die 4 Retter)

Fehlermeldungen im Diagramm: Nicht selbst debuggen! Fehlermeldung kopieren, in die KI einfügen und sagen: "Hier gab es einen Fehler, korrigieren."

Browser zeigt Code statt Dashboard: Deine Datei heißt unbemerkt cockpit.html.txt. Dateiendungen im Betriebssystem einblenden und das .txt löschen!

CORS-Blockade (Console zeigt Fehler): Dein Browser blockiert die Cloud-API aus Sicherheitsgründen. Nutze entweder eine lokale KI (Ollama) oder OpenRouter (openrouter.ai), die CORS-freundliche Endpunkte anbieten.

Layout zerbricht bei Mermaid: Die KI hat sich bei Sonderzeichen verschluckt. Textpassagen im Code einfach in doppelte Anführungszeichen "..." setzen.

Live-Demos & Linksammlung

Hier findest du alle fertigen Beispiele, Vorlagen und Tools aus dem Buch zum direkten Ausprobieren im Browser:

Linksammlung & Showcase (zentrale Anlaufstelle): the-zero-cost-ai-cockpit.github.io/Links

Showcase (Diagramm-Sammlung): the-zero-cost-ai-cockpit.github.io/Showcase

Demo-Cockpit (Basis Cockpit): the-zero-cost-ai-cockpit.github.io/Demo-Cockpit

Smartes Demo-Cockpit (API-Anbindung): the-zero-cost-ai-cockpit.github.io/Demo_Cockpit_smart

About the Author	

As an innovation-driven low-coder with a professional focus on Enterprise Architecture Management (EAM), Michael Estel uses this book to make digital transformation tangible for companies.

His dual academic qualifications (Master of Business Administration and Master in Educational Sciences) allow him to view challenges from different perspectives. Thus, he knows how to translate corporate strategies into operational KPIs and make these complex issues understandable and practically accessible to everyone.

He consistently drives forward the testing and application of his core passion – Artificial Intelligence – both privately and professionally. In this book, he bundles his practical experience from the IT and architecture environment and demonstrates how immediate added value – quick wins – can be generated through the targeted use of AI and open-source tools.

Michael Estel is convinced that true digital sovereignty does not stem from huge budgets, but arises where methodological know-how meets the right tools. His goal is to empower knowledge workers, project managers, managers, and consultants to shape the digital transformation in their projects actively, single-handedly, and in a resource-efficient manner.

Why This Book?

In a world flooded with data, rapid understanding is the decisive competitive advantage. Large corporations invest millions in Enterprise Architecture Management (EAM) and real-time dashboards to document their IT landscapes, steer processes, and make well-founded decisions.

Yet for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs), project managers, or specialized consultants, the established tools are often a massive obstacle. In their search for structure, they usually end up in one of two dead ends:

Financial overkill: Classic EAM solutions like SAP LeanIX, Bizzdesign, or Ardoq, as well as high-end dashboards like Abacum, Agicap, or Fathom, are powerful but rigid. They require months of onboarding, a dedicated IT department, and blow any budget with high monthly licensing costs. A custom dashboard from an IT consultant quickly runs into the five figures.

The "Doc Rot" chaos: Those who rely on standard office software instead quickly lose track. Microsoft Excel mutates into an error-prone battlefield of colorful cells that no one but its creator understands anymore. The result is "Doc Rot" (the decay of documentation) – outdated files gathering dust in digital drawers.

This book breaks that paradigm and shows you a pragmatic way out. The "0-Euro Cockpit" is not a mere software tutorial, but a new strategic blueprint for your business. We leverage the logic of large enterprises and combine strategic planning (process and architecture visualization) with the operational real-time transparency of a dashboard.

The best part: You don't have to pay a "software toll", you don't need a computer science degree, and you don't need a massive IT budget. The technical hurdle is much lower than you might expect: Anyone capable of logically structuring a PowerPoint presentation or using a simple SUM function in Excel can easily master this project. If you are ready to leave the role of a passive software consumer, you will become the "lead developer" here.

Everything you need to build your cockpit is ready to go in no time: Your system already comes with a web browser, a text editor, and the command line right out of the box. The only new tool we will bring in is the Ollama app for your local AI. Don't worry: In the command line, we work almost exclusively with simple copy-and-paste. The real magic is handled by language models (LLMs). They act as your personal translators. You simply describe your requirements precisely, and the AI builds the complex structures for you. 

The result is a tailor-made command center that:

Is cost-neutral: You use highly efficient open-source methods without monthly licensing fees.

Remains absolutely data-secure: You learn how to run the AI locally on your own computer using tools like Ollama. Sensitive financial data or strategy papers never leave your system.

Delivers enterprise-level quality: We use professional standards like BPMN 2.0 for processes and the C4 model for IT architectures. We save ourselves the tedious, manual modeling work: It is generated automatically by AI in seconds.

From the first local prompt to an automated, interactive cockpit – we are building a solution that normally costs five-figure sums. For 0 euros... Granted: the 0 euros only refer to the software costs – this book is, of course, worth the investment!

What the Cockpit Does for You

The "0-Euro Cockpit" that we are building here will become the central command center of your company. This brings you six tangible advantages:

Clarity instead of gut feeling: You build a local command center that provides you with exactly the figures and dependencies you need for solid decisions.

Complexity under control: Forget static PowerPoint sketches. You build models of your processes and IT systems that are logically linked to one another and cleverly adapt to changes.

Foundation for IT projects: Before you spend a lot of money on new software, you create the digital map of your business here. Anyone who knows their processes and data flows inside out will introduce systems like ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning – e.g., for accounting and materials management) or CRM (Customer Relationship Management – e.g., for sales) much more smoothly later on – and save on expensive consulting hours.

Fit for audits and major contracts: Whether it's ISO certification or proof of IT security – with clean process models, you demonstrate how professionally your business operates. This builds trust with major clients and authorities.

Full data control: You do not make yourself dependent on external cloud providers. Your sensitive business data remains exactly where it belongs: protected on your own hardware.

Centralized company knowledge: You create a single (internet-accessible) reliable source of information (Single Source of Truth). As a result, know-how is no longer just stored in the heads of individual employees, which, among other things, accelerates the onboarding of new colleagues.

Your Roadmap

The upcoming chapters interlock like gears to turn the project into reality step by step:

Chapter 1: The Foundation (Data Privacy & Local AI). Before we build, we lay the foundation. You will learn how to anonymize sensitive information and set up a GDPR-compliant, local AI environment using tools like Ollama, which runs completely offline on your computer. 

Result: You know which data you are allowed to process where – and you have your local AI ready to go.

Chapter 2: The Structure (AI Visualizes). Using the "diagram-as-code" approach, the AI translates your text descriptions into professional process workflows and structured IT architectures. 

Result: You have dynamic diagrams that update automatically when changes are made – without manual redrawing.

Chapter 3: The Network (AI Understands). Static images are just the beginning. You will learn how to build interactive knowledge graphs from your data that show you where hidden risks and bottlenecks lie at the push of a button. 

Result: You identify dependencies that remain invisible in spreadsheets – and can take proactive countermeasures.

Chapter 4: The Command Center (The Cockpit). We bring all the puzzle pieces together. The AI builds a single, locally executable HTML file from your diagrams, numbers, and graphs. Static data turns into your command center, which you can effortlessly feed with fresh numbers at any time via an integrated "dropzone" using drag-and-drop. 

Result: A functioning dashboard that you run without a server and update yourself.

● Chapter 5: The Intelligence (Make Your Cockpit Smart). We wake up your system. You will learn how to integrate the AI directly into your cockpit via so-called API interfaces, enabling it to independently analyze data, read documents, and update itself live. 

Result: Your cockpit evolves from a passive monitor into an active assistant.

Chapter 6: The Starting Gun (Taking Off with the Cockpit). To conclude, we bring your cockpit online, optimize the code structure for long-term maintainability, and take a look at automation options that will populate your system independently in the future. Result: You know how to use and further develop your cockpit within a team.

Tips for the Book

This is a practical guide: Fundamentally, this book has a modular structure. You can skim through it at any time and jump straight to the chapters or prompts you need for a current problem. However, my clear recommendation for maximum success is this: Start at the beginning, understand the systemic logic of the building blocks, and build your own cockpit step by step as you read. 

Time required: Expect 4-8 hours for the basic cockpit, depending on your familiarity with the tools.

Cockpit vs. Dashboard. A dashboard visualizes data and displays statuses. A cockpit goes one step further by bundling display, analysis, and options for action in one place. In Chapters 2–4, we first build the dashboard component and bring it to life through local drag-and-drop updates. In Chapter 5, we then expand it with smart AI features.

In Chapters 2–4, we first build the dashboard component and bring it to life through local drag-and-drop updates. In Chapter 5, we then expand it with smart AI features. 

The ultimate tip right up front: Let the AI think for you! A common beginner's mistake is to agonize for hours over the perfect command – the so-called "prompt". Save yourself this time. Instead, simply explain your goal to the AI and let it design the optimal prompt for you.

I want to [insert goal, e.g., build a digital company cockpit]. Write me the professional prompt that I can give to a coding AI so it implements this perfectly for me. 

Afterwards, you briefly check the result for content accuracy and apply it. Incidentally, the master prompt for our cockpit in this book was also created using this so-called "meta-prompting".

Use AI as a reading companion: If you want to dive deeper into a section or need help with implementation, simply copy the passage into your AI. It serves as a mentor that explains the book's content individually and adapts it to your specific requirements.

The Ping-Pong Principle: Always view the first code as a draft from a fast assistant. When replicating it, the moment will inevitably come when something doesn't work. That is normal and part of the process! Don't throw in the towel in frustration; instead, play "ping-pong": Point out the error to the machine in the chat and have it present the result to you again. Because only through your targeted feedback does a quick draft become a robust solution. 

An important note regarding the example prompts: If you rebuild your cockpit and copy the commands from this book 1:1, your result will likely differ slightly in detail from the examples here in the book. The reason for this is simple: Every AI model "thinks" a bit differently, and the underlying systems are evolving rapidly. Don't let that confuse you! You can rest assured that the basic functionality will be there and your requirements will be reliably implemented.

Chapter 1: Data Privacy & Local AI

Before we get started, we need to talk about the foundation: Your data. As the aspiring "lead developer" of your company, you bear the responsibility for what moves to the cloud and what remains securely in your own vault.

The GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) is often perceived as a "bureaucracy monster". At its core, however, it is just a checklist for professional work. It states: You may only process personal data if you have a legal basis.

When are you allowed to upload personal data?

Consent: If your customers or employees have explicitly agreed (e.g., through an addendum in your T&Cs or contracts).

Legitimate interest: For purely organizational processes without sensitive details (like finances), this is often justifiable – although, technically speaking, the data still leaves your direct control upon upload.

This presentation does not replace legal advice. In case of doubt, consult your data protection officer!

To ensure your cockpit doesn't become a data leak, you should use the following options:

1.1 Anonymization

Use the oldest trick in IT: Search & Replace (Ctrl + H). Swap "Müller GmbH" for "Customer_A" or "Ms. Schmidt" for "Contact_Person_1". The AI doesn't need real names to understand logic – it only needs the structure. What is not uploaded cannot be leaked.

1.2 Digital Muzzle 

Almost all major AI providers use your chats to train their models by default. You don't want that for your business. Here is how you prevent it:

The ChatGPT Check:

Go to Settings -> Data controls and disable "Chat history & training".

Pro tip: Use the "Temporary Chat". Here, inputs are not used for either training or personalization.

The Google Check (Gemini & AI Studio):

Gemini (Web): Go to gemini.google.com, open "Activity", and turn off "Save activity".

Google AI Studio (aistudio.google.com): Check the data processing options in the settings. Pay attention to whether your data may be used for "product improvement" and actively opt out of it.

Workspace (Gmail/Drive): In the Gmail settings under "Smart features and personalization", disable data usage for Google services.

Beware the GDPR Trap: Just because "training" is turned off doesn't mean the system is automatically 100% GDPR-compliant. Data often flows through US servers. For highly sensitive customer data, this is only the second-best solution.

Ask yourself the following question before every upload: Would it be bad if this information were in the newspaper tomorrow?

If YES: Use a local AI or anonymize consistently before copying.
completed.3:
If YES: Use a local AI or anonymize consistently before copying.

If NO: Use the cloud, but make sure training is disabled.

Do you fall into the "If YES" category, or are you simply curious? Then the following option is fundamental for you:

1.3 Local AI

If you are working with bank details, calculations, or sensitive strategy papers, the cloud is not an option for you. The solution to this dilemma is called Ollama (ollama.com).

With it, you install the Artificial Intelligence directly on your own hardware. Not a single piece of information leaves your system. You retain full data sovereignty.

1.3.1 Ollama Desktop App 

For your first contact with local AI, you can use the Ollama App (ollama.com/download). Download the package for macOS or Windows and install it as usual.

This version offers you a graphical interface (similar to ChatGPT) and allows you to conveniently process documents via drag-and-drop. This means it doesn't just understand text, but can also analyze PDFs, spreadsheets, or screenshots (depending on the model).

Use Case: Anonymizing Data: This desktop app is our tool of choice for getting started. Before we build complex cockpit structures later on, we must ensure that sensitive raw data is sanitized. Using it is simple:

Open the app.

Simply drag and drop your sensitive file (e.g., an Excel list or PDF) into the chat window.

Enter the command: 

Strictly anonymize the following text. Replace all personal data with functional placeholders 

(e.g., [NAME_1], [ADDRESS_1], [DATE_1], [TITLE_1]). 

Rules:

Consistency: The same entity always receives the same placeholder.

Readability: Preserve sentence structure and context.

Completeness: Remove names, locations, professions, contact details, and specific IDs.

Text: [PASTE DATA OR TEXT HERE]

The AI now takes over the work locally for you – securely and discreetly.

The Ollama Desktop Application

1.3.2 Which Model is Right for Me?

The app alone is just the chassis – you still need the "engine", the so-called model. The numbers behind the names (e.g., 3B, 7B, 14B) describe the size of this engine (parameters in billions). Before we get started, let's briefly find the right model for your use case:

3B to 4B: Nimble sprinters. They run smoothly on almost any modern laptop and are perfectly sufficient for most cockpit tasks (our all-rounder tip: qwen3.5:4b).

7B to 9B: The "gold standard". Noticeably smarter with complex contexts, but require more computing power.

14B+: The heavyweights for deep data analysis.

You can find an overview of all available models and their run commands directly in the Ollama Model Library (ollama.com/library). Most models come in various sizes. Modern models like Qwen 3.5 (ollama.com/library/qwen3.5) or Gemma 4 (ollama.com/library/gemma4) are very efficient, intelligent, and multimodal – meaning they can also process images.

The Hardware Check: You don't need an office data center for local AI. As little as 16 GB of RAM and a graphics card with 4 GB of VRAM (e.g., GTX 1650) are enough to run the small models (3B to 4B) stably. If you want to work smoothly with the larger models, 8 GB of VRAM (e.g., RTX 3060) or a current Apple Mac with an M-chip and unified memory are exactly what you need.

1.3.3 Ollama via Command Line 

The desktop app is wonderful for a quick start and anonymization. However, for the following chapters of this book, we will need Ollama on a slightly deeper level. To automate our smart cockpit later and steer the AI's responses precisely, the app's interface is no longer sufficient. 

Once you have installed the Ollama app, the system is automatically available via your computer's command line as well. And this is exactly where we will primarily work to bring our cockpit to life later!

A brief word about the command line - found as "Terminal" on Windows and macOS: You don't need any special IT skills for this. 1.3.4 The Hybrid Strategy

Finally, we put the puzzle pieces together. Why the effort with Ollama and the terminal? Because we run a hybrid strategy in a business context. Security meets speed – utilize the strengths of both worlds:

Sanitize locally: You feed your actual raw data into Ollama locally on your computer (e.g., via the desktop app) and have it anonymized in compliance with data privacy regulations.

Think big: You then copy these clean, "harmless" facts into the powerful cloud models (like Gemini or ChatGPT) without any data privacy concerns, to let them generate the complex content for your cockpit.

This way, you maintain full control over sensitive data at all times, without having to forgo the computing power of the tech giants!

1.4 The Most Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them

Overburdening hardware

The problem: A massive 14B model brings a standard laptop to its knees. The fans howl, the system freezes, and answers arrive at a snail's pace.

The solution: Always start with lean models in the 3B to 4B class (e.g., qwen3.5:4b). Only switch to larger models when the performance is no longer sufficient for the task. You can find models in the Ollama Library (ollama.com/library).

Drag-and-Drop in the terminal

The problem: Dragging Word or PDF files directly into the command line window doesn't work and generates errors.

The solution: Use copy-and-paste for plain text in the terminal, though depending on the model, you can also drag images in. For processing entire documents, you should use a graphical interface like Open WebUI (openwebui.com).

Data without clear guidance 

The problem: You throw raw data at the AI without telling it what to do. The AI guesses, loses the thread, and begins to "hallucinate" (invent things).

The solution: Always state the role and instruction first (e.g., "You are a data privacy assistant. Anonymize the following text:"). Visually separate the command and the data, ideally with dashes (---).

Blowing up the AI's memory

The problem: You copy 50 pages of text into the chat at once. The AI ignores the beginning or breaks off in the middle because its "short-term memory" (Context Window) is full.

The solution: Feed the model in digestible chunks and break large datasets down into smaller sections.

Ignoring the language barrier

The problem: You write in German, but the AI answers clumsily or switches to English. Many models are primarily trained on English.

The solution: Specifically use multilingual models that, even in compact variants, have excellent command of German.

1.5 Conclusion

Data privacy and AI usage are not mutually exclusive – they merely require the right strategy. With local models via Ollama acting as your "digital bouncer" and the hybrid method for complex tasks, you have now poured a secure foundation. You retain full sovereignty over your data and effortlessly navigate around expensive GDPR traps.

2.2 Of Architectures and IT Landscapes 

An end to the guesswork during system failures: We will create a digital development plan of your business. You will visualize the dependencies between hardware, software, and cloud services as precisely as a major corporation. This allows you to maintain full control over your data flows with every software change or onboarding.

2.2.1 Why an IT Diagram?

System failure (The invisible error): A connection tool (such as Zapier) fails in the background. What happens? Without a clear IT diagram, you are poking around in the fog. Because your online shop (e.g., Shopify) continues to run undisturbed and customers are busy shopping. At first, you have no idea that the orders are no longer being forwarded to your warehouse and accounting is no longer writing invoices. The cash register rings at the front, while your processes collapse at the back – and you only notice it when the first customers complain.

Software change (The hidden connections): A salesperson convinces you to introduce a new, fancy program for your customer data (CRM). What he doesn't tell you in the sales pitch: If you simply switch off the old system, you unknowingly also sever the automatic connection to your support inbox. Suddenly, customer service no longer knows which customer bought what. A quick software switch turns into a weeks-long repair operation because hidden dependencies were destroyed.

A single, clean IT diagram would have explained this entire structure to her at a glance in five seconds.

2.2.2 The C4 Model 

The rescue from this chaos is an IT development plan. The C4 model (c4model.com) is suitable here. C4 stands for: Context, Container, Components, Code. A single glance at such a context diagram in your dashboard immediately shows you all systems and their dependencies. Let me explain it to you:

Just imagine a Google Maps map:

1. Context (The globe): Your farm in the middle and all the external systems around it that it communicates with.

2. Container (The city map): You zoom into the system. Now you see the rough building blocks: the web app, the database, and the mobile app. Here you can see which technologies (e.g., Java or SQL) are used.

3. Components (The building plan): You zoom into a container (e.g., the web app). Now you see the individual functional blocks inside, such as the "login service" or the "email dispatch".

4. Code (The floor plan): This is the magnifying glass. Here you see the actual classes or functions. In practice, this level is often omitted because it changes too quickly.

Practice shows: For most small and medium-sized enterprises, this first level – the system context – is completely sufficient as a strategic basis. The deeper levels (Container, Components, Code) usually only become relevant when you have your own software developed. 

What we build here in a few minutes via prompt is a building block of Enterprise Architecture Management (EAM) – and without the otherwise usual, tedious manual system and modeling work. You adapt proven architecture standards from the corporate environment for your business – pragmatically and entirely without expensive software licenses.

For the implementation, you use the following components:

PlantUML: A text-based language for diagrams – particularly strong in IT architecture. You can render (visualize) the code directly here: plantuml.com.

diagrams.net (formerly draw.io): A multifunctional drawing tool that can also import PlantUML code. 

2.2.3 Creating Your IT Diagram

Step 1: Write down what hardware and software you use. A photo of your notes will be sufficient input for the AI later.

Step 2: Create PlantUML code via AI:

Here is my data: [your text/photo]

System_Ext(billbee, "Billbee", "Manages inventory and shipping.")

System_Ext(zendesk, "Zendesk", "Customer service and ticket system.")

System_Ext(lexoffice, "Lexoffice", "Cloud accounting.")

' Relationships

Rel(sarah, it_infrastruktur, "Uses for control & communication")

Rel(it_infrastruktur, shopify, "Manages products & shop design")

Rel(shopify, zapier, "Sends new order data")

Rel(zapier, billbee, "Forwards orders to warehouse")

Rel(zapier, zendesk, "Creates customer profiles for new purchases")

Rel(billbee, lexoffice, "Transfers completed invoice data")

@enduml

2.5 The Most Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them

Before we jump into the next chapter, here is some quick troubleshooting. When you handle generated code, something will inevitably get stuck at some point. That is completely normal and part of the process. With these four tricks, you can save the situation in seconds:

Error messages instead of diagrams: You pasted the code, and instead of a chic diagram, the editor just spits out error messages? Don't panic. You don't have to learn to debug this code yourself. 

Solution: Simply copy the entire error message, throw it into the chat with your AI and say: "There was an error here, please correct it." The machine will apologize and deliver the repaired code to you.

The special character diva: Mermaid in particular can sometimes react extremely cranky to unexpected brackets or exotic special characters in the text. If the layout suddenly breaks, this is often the cause. 

Solution: Simply enclose the affected text in the code in double quotes. The AI theoretically knows this itself, but sometimes forgets – here you are the supervisor.

The secret update: You open your cockpit and suddenly the diagrams are gone? Tools like Mermaid are evolving rapidly. If you always load the latest version via the CDN (Content Delivery Network) in your HTML code, an update from the publisher can break your code.

Solution: Always ask the AI in your prompt to include a fixed version (e.g., 11.x). Just research this online beforehand.

The monster diagram: Don't even try to squeeze your company's entire workflows of the last ten years into a single chart. The result resembles an exploded circuit diagram and is not helpful in everyday life. 

Solution: Start small! A process with 5 to 7 individual steps is perfect. If it gets more complex, cut the process and outsource the rest to a second diagram.

The overzealous assistant (AI hallucinations): When faced with confusing data, AIs often creatively fill in gaps instead of admitting ignorance. Such invented values falsify your dashboard and lead to wrong decisions. 

The solution: Build a "seatbelt" into your prompt: 

IMPORTANT: If you don't understand data or find gaps, do not guess. Do not fill in anything on your own, but ask me specifically.

This prevents blind guessing and secures your data quality.

Data loss in the browser: Web editors like the Mermaid Live Editor, the PlantUML Web Server, or BPMN.io do not save reliably. If you accidentally close the tab or reload the page, your masterpiece has often vanished into digital nirvana. 

Solution: Make it a habit to always save the generated text code in parallel in a simple local text file, or export the finished diagram immediately as an image.

2.6 Conclusion

"Diagram-as-Code" is your tool to finally make complex workflows visible – without the tedious shifting of individual image elements. This makes outdated documentation ("Doc Rot") a thing of the past: If a process changes, you simply adjust the text and the diagram updates itself.

However, as elegant and efficient as this automated visualization is, it marks only the first milestone. Ultimately, a diagram is made for the human eye: It provides clear answers, but only for the specific snippet you programmed. The information it contains remains bound to that one chart.

To fully exploit the potential of your company knowledge, we must break open these documentary boundaries. In the next chapter, we therefore change perspective: We take the decisive step from drawn structure to a continuous database and enter the next evolutionary stage: The Knowledge Graph.

You have documented your processes and mapped the IT systems – that's the mandatory part. The freestyle begins where you understand dependencies beyond pure IT. In every company, an invisible network exists: Who communicates with whom? Which tool feeds which system? What happens if node X breaks away?

If a central employee drops out or a system crashes, you must immediately grasp the chain reaction. This so-called "blast radius" can hardly be captured in rigid spreadsheets. This is where the knowledge graph comes into play.

A knowledge graph is an intelligent map of your data. While Excel "locks up" information in rows and columns, a graph connects it into a dynamic network.

The concept:

Nodes: The objects (e.g., employee Max, tool Excel, property A).

Edges: The relationships between them (e.g., "Max manages property A").

A knowledge graph is so powerful because it doesn't lock information into rigid tables, but organizes it – much like our own brain – as a living network of associations and links. This makes it the ideal foundation for smart enterprise architecture.

A concrete example: Imagine Carsten, the clever consultant. He is analyzing the IT infrastructure and supply chains for a medium-sized client. On the management's endless Excel lists, everything looks stable, and they think they have everything under control. However, when Carsten transfers this data into an interactive knowledge graph, the "aha" moment follows during a joint workshop:

A node called "Old Merchandise Management System (Legacy)" turns out to be a massive bottleneck at the center of the graph. Almost 80% of all processes – from ordering materials to invoicing – run unnoticed through this single, outdated server. If this system fails, everything in the company comes to a sudden halt. This risk (a so-called "single point of failure") was completely invisible in the flat spreadsheets – in the graph, it is immediately obvious to everyone. Carsten's strategic recommendation from today on: Prioritize modernizing exactly this bottleneck, instead of burning budget on side battlefields.

The graph not only provides Carsten and his client with an overview of the interdependencies but also serves as a true strategic early warning system.

Carsten has provided an "aha" moment at Maschinenbau AG. 

Now I will show you how you can create your own knowledge graph.

4.3.3 The Dropzone Way: Updating Data via Drag-and-Drop

You have now learned two ways to keep your cockpit up to date: the AI way for larger rebuilds (Chapter 4.3.1) and the do-it-yourself way for minor text changes (Chapter 4.3.2). Both have their justification – but for the absolute classic, namely the regular importing of fresh numbers, there is an even more elegant solution.

Consider the following situation: At the end of the month, you export your latest account transactions from online banking. You neither want to painstakingly have the new numbers integrated by AI (and possibly send sensitive bank data to the cloud in the process), nor do you want to manually replace 80 new values in the HTML code.

Exactly for this situation, we are now building a dropzone (a filing zone for files) directly into your cockpit.

The Gas Cap Analogy Imagine your cockpit like a car: The body, the steering wheel, the instruments – everything is fixed. But when the tank is empty, you don't rebuild the entire car. You simply open the gas cap and fill up with fresh fuel. That is exactly what we are doing: You drag and drop your current Excel or CSV file into your cockpit, and the KPI tiles and charts recalculate themselves automatically. No AI in the background, no cloud, no API key – everything happens 100% locally in your browser.

The functional principle in 4 steps: Behind this apparent magic lies a simple chain:

The Drop: You drag the file into the dropzone. The browser registers the file.

The FileReader: This is a built-in function of your browser that reads files locally – without uploading them anywhere.

The Parsing: A small JavaScript library transforms the raw data of your Excel spreadsheet into a clean, machine-readable data package.

The Re-rendering: Your diagrams (e.g., Chart.js) redraw themselves automatically with the fresh numbers.

The crucial part: Only milliseconds pass between Step 1 and 4 – and not a single data packet leaves your computer.

Your New Tools To ensure the browser understands the tables, we need two small, free code libraries, which we embed directly into your HTML file via CDN (Content Delivery Network) as usual:

SheetJS (xlsx): Reliably reads Excel files (.xlsx, .xls).

PapaParse: The gold standard for processing CSV files flawlessly.

Important note: Remember the tip from Chapter 2.4 – instruct the AI to always embed a fixed version of the libraries. Otherwise, a silent update by the publishers can paralyze your cockpit.

The prompt for your drag-and-drop upgrade Simply open the AI chat in which you built your cockpit, upload your current cockpit.html, and give the AI this master assignment:

Expand my existing cockpit.html with a local drag-and-drop function for data updating – entirely without an API connection.

DROPZONE: Build a clearly visible dropzone above the KPI tiles (dashed border, matching the design). It should provide visual feedback when a file hovers over it.

FILE FORMATS: Support .xlsx, .xls, and .csv. Embed SheetJS and PapaParse via CDN with a fixed version number for this purpose.

LOCAL PARSING: Read the file exclusively via FileReader in the browser. No upload to a server. No API calls.

DATA MAPPING: The file contains typical columns such as "Date", "Category", and "Amount". Program logic that automatically sums the amounts per category.

If several things don't fit, change them individually as well. Test every small adjustment immediately in JSFiddle.

The wrong file extensions. Your browser only shows confusing text instead of a cockpit? Often file extensions are hidden in the system and you unknowingly save a pure text file.

Solution: Exactly as discussed in Chapter 3.4: Make the file extensions visible in your system so that your secret .txt finally becomes a real .html.

Fear of the code. You don't dare to change a single word in the HTML, for fear of destroying the entire system.

Solution: Use your sandbox at JSFiddle. You can break absolutely nothing there. Pressing Ctrl + Z (Undo) repairs any mistake immediately. Just go for it!

The column name trap during dropzone upload. Your exported Excel file suddenly calls the column "Amount (EUR)", but your code stubbornly looks for "Amount". The script finds nothing, and the diagrams remain empty after dragging the file in.

Solution: Always keep the exact same column names in your exports from the bank or CRM. Alternatively: Ask the AI in your prompt to recognize the column names in the code flexibly ("fuzzy"), so that variations are ignored. 

The comma-semicolon catastrophe. German Excel versions often export CSV files with a semicolon as a separator, but international tools (like PapaParse) expect a comma by default. After the drop, nothing happens at all.

Solution: It is best to save your tables directly as a regular Excel file (.xlsx); this format problem does not exist there. If you must use CSV, add this to your prompt: 

"Configure PapaParse to automatically detect delimiters."

The invisible date trap. Excel often saves dates internally as sequential numbers (e.g., 45627 instead of 15.11.2026). After the drop, your cockpit then displays completely confusing numbers on the chart axes.

Solution: If you want to evaluate dates in the cockpit, build this sentence into your master prompt as a safeguard: "Automatically convert Excel date formats into a readable format (ISO) when reading them out."

The AI breaks off in the middle: The AI generates your code, but suddenly stops in the middle of a line or bracket.

The solution: This is not an error; the AI has simply reached its maximum response length (the so-called token limit). Do not click "Regenerate"! Write in the chat: 

Continue writing the code exactly from the point where you left off.

Then seamlessly copy both code parts together into your text file.

5.1 The API Integration

To make your cockpit smart, we use so-called APIs (Application Programming Interfaces). Imagine an API like a waiter in a restaurant: Your cockpit (the guest) places an order – for example, "Here is a text, analyze it" or "Give me the current account balance". The waiter (the API) carries this request to the kitchen (the AI or a database) and brings the finished result back to you.

To make your cockpit smart, we use so-called APIs (Application Programming Interfaces). Imagine an API like a waiter in a restaurant: Your cockpit (the guest) places an order – for example, "Here is a text, analyze it" or "Give me the current account balance". The waiter (the API) carries this request to the kitchen (the AI or a database) and brings the finished result back to you.

For this, your browser uses a built-in JavaScript function called "fetch" (more on this at developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Fetch_API). This allows your cockpit to communicate with the outside world completely silently in the background, without the page ever having to reload.

5.1.1 Where Do I Find My API Key?

To send out your digital "waiter", you need to generate a free key (a long string of characters) once from the respective cloud provider. Here is where you can find it:

Google Gemini: Go to Google AI Studio (aistudio.google.com) and click on "Get API key" on the left, then select "Create API key" and copy it.

OpenAI (ChatGPT): Log in to the OpenAI Platform (platform.openai.com), click on "Create new secret key", and copy it.

OpenRouter: Register at openrouter.ai/keys and click on "Create Key".

Attention: API usage is generally subject to a fee. However, some providers offer a free tier – such as Google via the AI Studio free tier or OpenRouter with selected free models. That should be enough for building your cockpit. With regular, continuous use of affordable models, experience shows you should expect costs in the low single-digit Euro range per month.

Local (Ollama): Here you don't need a classic key, as the AI safely resides on your own computer anyway. But for your local browser cockpit to be allowed to speak with Ollama at all (without being blocked by the browser's internal security rules), we use the universal start command with the asterisk (*):

Windows: Open PowerShell and enter this command: 

$env:OLLAMA_ORIGINS="*"; ollama serve

macOS: Open Terminal and enter this command: 

OLLAMA_ORIGINS="*" ollama serve

Security tip: The "Ignition Key" rule. The asterisk (*) in this command is extremely convenient, but it also means that, theoretically, any website with malicious intent that you currently have open in your browser could access your local AI. Therefore, make it a habit, just like driving a car: When you work in your cockpit, you put the key in the ignition (you start the command in the terminal). When you finish work, you take the key out again – you simply press Ctrl + C in your black window and close it. As soon as Ollama is terminated, your computer is 100% "locked" again.

The Offline Trick: If that's too sketchy for you, there is a safe way: Briefly disconnect your computer from the Wi-Fi or unplug the network cable for the time you are working on your cockpit. Where there is no internet, there is no danger from the outside.

Excursion for the Brave: Your Own Mini-Server Anyone who wants to delve a little deeper into the developer sphere and solve the security problem completely professionally uses a trick: You don't start your cockpit with a double-click, but turn it into a "real" little website. If you don't have Python on your computer yet, just download it for free at python.org/downloads. Important tip for Windows users: When installing, be sure to check the small box for "Add Python to PATH" right at the beginning! 

1. Start Ollama with the secure command that only allows your computer:

Windows: 

$env:OLLAMA_ORIGINS="http://localhost:*"; ollama serve

macOS: 

OLLAMA_ORIGINS="http://localhost*" ollama serve

2. Open a second black window exactly in the folder where your HTML file is located:

Windows: Hold down the Shift key, right-click in the empty area of the folder, and select "Open in Terminal" (or PowerShell).

macOS: Open a new Terminal. Type cd (important: with a space at the end!) and simply drag your cockpit folder with the mouse directly into the window. Then press Enter.

3. Start the server (type the command and press Enter):

Windows: 

python -m http.server 8000

macOS: 

python3 -m http.server 8000 (Macs always need the "3" in the command)

4. Open your web browser and type into the address bar at the top: 

http://localhost:8000/cockpit.html

That's it! Your browser now recognizes the cockpit as a regular website, and the Ollama bouncer waves you through – because only your own computer (localhost) is permitted, not the whole world anymore. But don't worry: Your cockpit from Chapter 5 will also work without this Python server!

You don't need any deep programming knowledge to build this into your cockpit.html yourself. You just have to tell the AI to program this flexible "waiter" for you. For this, you need a basic prompt like this one:

The generated HTML/JS code must be absolutely robust and strictly adhere to the following architectural specifications:

Central Provider Configuration: Create a global JavaScript object for the providers (OpenAI, Google Gemini, OpenRouter, Ollama) incl. their respective standard base URLs. Build dropdown menus into the user interface for providers and models, a field for the API key, as well as a button for an API connection test.

The Simultaneous Translator: Write an intelligent buildRequest and an extractResponse function. These must dynamically adapt the fetch payload and the extraction of the JSON response to the format of the selected provider (e.g., Ollama's data.response vs. OpenAI's data.choices[0].message.content).

Robust JSON Extractor: AIs often respond with Markdown blocks (e.g., json ... ). It is mandatory to write a regex helper function extractJSON() that reliably extracts the pure JSON string from the text before JSON.parse().

File Handling in the Browser: Read dropped files purely locally via the FileReader API. Send this content asychronously via fetch to the API using a system prompt.

UI & Error Handling: Do not use alert() for feedback (success/error), but rather program a modern, visual toast notification system. Also show a loading indicator during the request.

DOM Update: Formulate the system prompt so that the API exclusively answers with structured JSON. Use the extracted JSON to update the KPI tiles in the cockpit live via DOM manipulation.

Deliver exclusively the finished, clean, and functioning HTML/JS code without additional Markdown explanations.

Why is this prompt so long and technical? Don't worry, you don't have to master technical terms like regex or JSON extractor yourself. This prompt is basically your digital site manager. It forbids the AI from amateur "hobby tinkering" and instead forces it to program your system at a professional level.

In short, this safety net ensures three things:

No code salad: The AI is forced to format its answers so cleanly that your cockpit doesn't crash when reading data (the extractor does this).

Limitless flexibility: Whether you use Google, OpenAI, or local models, your cockpit translates everything automatically in the background so that any AI can dock immediately (the simultaneous translator).

True professional look: Instead of annoying gray pop-ups, you get modern loading bars and subtle info messages (the toast system).

5.1.4 Hardcoding the API Key into the Code?

When building your cockpit, this thought might quickly come to mind: "It's pretty annoying to enter the long API key over and over again. Can't I just tell the AI to hardcode the key into the code?"

The short answer is: Yes, theoretically you could adapt your prompt so that the cockpit works 'out of the box' immediately.

But for security reasons, I strongly advise against it!

 The "Postcard" Rule: HTML files are like open books. An API key directly in the code of your cockpit is not visible to the normal user at first glance, but it is never truly hidden.

Anyone who gets their hands on this file (be it a colleague you proudly show your cockpit to, or someone you accidentally send the file to) can read your key with a simple press of the F12 key via the browser developer tools. It's like writing your bank card PIN on a postcard.

The consequence: Anyone who has the file can steal your API key and happily chat with the AI at your expense (or your quota).

The golden rule: Stick with the version featuring the input field in your cockpit. That is the secure way – especially if you are not the only one who will be using the cockpit.

If the screen stays white or there is no response, it is usually due to one of these five points. Here is how you solve them in seconds:

1. CORS Blockages (The Browser Bouncer)

The problem: For security reasons, your local HTML file (file://) is often not allowed to communicate directly with cloud APIs. The F12 console reports a "CORS policy" error.

6.1 Architecture Update

The current cockpit.html bundles everything into a single file. This is technically necessary for getting started: If you open the cockpit locally by double-clicking it, the browser would block multiple separate HTML files for security reasons. But this monolithic block becomes confusing over time. If you just want to make a small change, you have to scroll through thousands of lines of code – that costs nerves and creates sources of error.

The solution is the "divide and conquer" principle. There are two approaches here, depending on your deployment location.

6.1.1 Modularizing Code (for Local Use)

Since we cannot cut it into real files for local use, we instruct the AI to sort the code within the single file into isolated modules – like drawers in a cabinet. This is strictly separated into three levels:

Layout (HTML): At the top lies the basic framework – header, navigation, content area, and footer. This "picture frame" remains unchanged.

Design (CSS): All formatting is bundled centrally in the <style> area. If you want to change the color scheme, you don't search through the whole document, but only adapt this one block.

Function (JavaScript): The logic – navigation, API calls, interactions – resides in the <script> area. This is clearly separated from the content.

Data Blocks: At the end of the file, all content goes into clearly commented sections. Each area is marked with instructions like <!-- INSERT YOUR DATA HERE -->.

Using the cockpit.html as an example, the blocks would look as follows:

HTML: Defines the layout and the basic structure of the page.

CSS: Controls the design, colors, and visual presentation.

JavaScript: Contains the interactive functions and application logic.

Data Blocks: Contains the actual content, clearly commented.

The advantage: You still only have a single file. You can put it on a USB stick, send it by email, or open it locally – it works immediately on any computer with a double-click. No browser blocks, no server configuration.

6.1.2 Dismantling the Cockpit (for Server Hosting)

As soon as your cockpit is on a server – be it on GitHub Pages, a classic web hosting provider, or on your own Raspberry Pi – the browser's technical restrictions no longer apply. Via HTTP or HTTPS, you can then easily load separate CSS, JavaScript, and HTML files.

The advantages at a glance:

Maintainability: Changes to the design only affect the CSS file, logic changes only the JavaScript file. You no longer have to search through a huge HTML file.

Teamwork: Colleagues can work on individual pages without destroying each other's code. Everyone takes care of their module.

Load time: The browser only loads the files needed for the current view. This makes your cockpit noticeably faster.

Expandability: You add new functions as a separate file – without touching the basic framework. Do you want a ticket page? Simply create tickets.html.

Automation: External tools like n8n or OpenClaw can update CSV files in the same folder without touching the HTML structure. This is the key to true automation.

6.2 Providing the Cockpit Online

Your cockpit works perfectly locally. But if you work in a team, you all want to access the same, up-to-date data. The central command must therefore go online.

Before you begin, you must know an important ground rule of the internet: If you want to call up your cockpit as a website, you absolutely must rename your main file to index.html. Web servers are programmed to load exactly this file as the start page. If your file is named something else (like cockpit.html), the server will not recognize it and your screen will remain blank.

Now we face a problem well-known in the industry: Simply uploading a website is often free today – but as soon as you want to put a secure "door lock" (password protection) in front of it, almost all providers suddenly demand money.

An extremely important warning upfront: If you ask an AI how to password-protect an HTML file for free, it will often advise you to use a small JavaScript code that displays a password window upon opening. Never do this! This is purely "fake protection". Anyone who right-clicks in the browser and selects "View Page Source" immediately sees all your financial and process data, as well as the password, in plain text.

Imagine this: A new tradesperson receipt comes in via email. The OpenClaw agent reads the email, extracts the costs, allocates them to the correct cost center, and writes the new total directly into the CSV file of your GitHub repository. The agent then performs a so-called "commit & push" completely autonomously – it officially saves the change, so to speak, and uploads it independently.

GitHub saves the updated CSV file. The next time it is accessed, your cockpit.html reads this file and displays the updated charts. The next time you or your employees open the page, the charts are already up to date. No manual entry, no waiting time. 

This fully automated Git integration brings true enterprise architecture level to your projects – almost as seamlessly and invisibly. In short: The AI does all the prep work and follow-up.

But beware:

True autonomy brings not only freedom but also significant security risks. An agent that independently reads emails and publishes code requires a clean setup. Here you leave the beginner zone and have to delve much deeper into the technical matter. If your curiosity is piqued and you want to see what the future of automation looks like, it is best to take a look straight into the documentation: docs.openclaw.ai/start/getting-started

Your journey continues

You have the tools, you have the AI by your side – now it is up to you what you build from it. Stay curious, continuously learn to dive deeper into the developer sphere, test things out, and above all: Keep control over your data and your business.

6.4 Conclusion and Closing

Look back for a moment and remember where you stood on the first pages of this book: With the frustration over confusing spreadsheets, rigid processes, and expensive standard software that squeezed you into a corset and never entirely did what your daily business operations actually demanded of it.
